threats.title: Unpinned GitHub Action Tag Reference (codeql-analysis.yml)
threats.scenario: `codeql-analysis.yml:23`, `codeql-analysis.yml:34`, and `codeql-analysis.yml:36` reference `github/codeql-action/init@v3`, `autobuild@v3`, and `analyze@v3` via mutable tag references. The `@v3` tag is a pointer the upstream maintainer can move to any commit at any time. An attacker who compromises the `github/codeql-action` repository (via a maintainer account, a malicious PR merged through a weak review process, or a tag-pointer rewrite) can redirect `@v3` to a malicious commit. On the next CI run, the pipeline executes that code with `permissions: security-events: write`, `actions: read`, and `contents: read` — enough to suppress findings, exfiltrate `GITHUB_TOKEN`, or persist a backdoor in the security-events feed. All three actions run on every `push` and `pull_request`, including from public forks.
threats.evidence_summary: `github/codeql-action/init@v3`, `autobuild@v3`, and `analyze@v3` at lines 23, 34, and 36 use mutable `@v3` tag references instead of commit SHA pins.
threats.impact_description: A compromised `@v3` tag allows arbitrary code execution in the CI runner with repository token privileges, enabling secret exfiltration, suppression of security findings, or artifact tampering.
threats.controls_in_place: All other third-party actions in the same workflow files are SHA-pinned. The CodeQL action steps are the specific outlier.
threats.mitigation_title: Pin all github/codeql-action steps to commit SHA in codeql-analysis.yml

steps: For each `uses: github/codeql-action/*@v3` step in `.github/workflows/codeql-analysis.yml`, replace the tag reference with the full 40-character commit SHA of the current `v3` tag tip, e.g. `uses: github/codeql-action/init@<sha> # v3.27.0`.

Enable Dependabot for GitHub Actions (`dependabot.yml` under `.github/`) with `package-ecosystem: github-actions` so SHA pins are auto-updated with version bumps. Also pin `coverallsapp/github-action@v2` in `ci.yml:161` using the same pattern.
remediation.code_example:
# .github/workflows/codeql-analysis.yml
- name: Initialize CodeQL
  uses: github/codeql-action/init@b9e3a9f0f67b9b3a9f0f67b9b3a9f0f67b9b3a9f #v3.27.0
  with:
    languages: ${{ matrix.language }}

threats.title: Disabled Lockfile Enforcement Allows Dependency Substitution (.npmrc)
threats.scenario: `.npmrc` contains `package-lock=false`, which instructs npm to skip writing and reading `package-lock.json`. When `npm install` runs in CI (e.g. `ci.yml:85`, `ci.yml:125`, `ci.yml:211`), npm resolves dependency versions fresh from the registry each time, without a pinned lockfile to enforce reproducibility. A dependency that publishes a malicious patch version within the declared semver range (e.g. `*`, `^x.y.z`, `~x.y.z`) is silently installed on the next CI run. Because `postinstall` hooks execute during the same `npm install` (see `package.json:88`), the injected version runs arbitrary code in the CI runner with access to all environment secrets. This attack pattern (dependency confusion / version squatting) requires no write access to the repo — only a published package in the npm registry satisfying the version range.
threats.evidence_summary: `.npmrc:1` sets `package-lock=false`, disabling lockfile generation and reproducible installs across all CI jobs that call `npm install` without `--ignore-scripts`.
threats.impact_description: A malicious transitive dependency published to npm within an existing semver range executes arbitrary code during CI builds, with access to `DOCKERHUB_TOKEN`, `HEROKU_API_KEY`, `CYPRESS_RECORD_KEY`, and `GITHUB_TOKEN`.
threats.controls_in_place: The `lint` and `lint-fixer` jobs use `--ignore-scripts`, preventing hook execution in those specific jobs. No lockfile pins are enforced for the test, e2e, or docker publish jobs.
threats.mitigation_title: Remove package-lock=false from .npmrc and commit a lockfile for CI

steps: Remove the `package-lock=false` line from `.npmrc`. Run `npm install` locally to generate `package-lock.json`, commit it, and push.

Update all CI jobs that run `npm install` without `--ignore-scripts` (`ci.yml:85`, `ci.yml:125`, `ci.yml:211`, `release.yml:41`) to use `npm ci` instead of `npm install`. `npm ci` enforces the lockfile and fails if `package-lock.json` is absent or inconsistent, giving deterministic dependency resolution on every build.
remediation.code_example:
# .github/workflows/ci.yml — replace npm install with npm ci in all test jobs
- name: "Install application"
  run: npm ci    # fails if package-lock.json is missing or out of date

threats.title: Unsafe Postinstall Hook Execution During Production Docker Build (Dockerfile)
threats.scenario: `Dockerfile:5` runs `npm install --omit=dev --unsafe-perm`. The `--unsafe-perm` flag disables npm's safety restriction that would otherwise drop privileges during lifecycle scripts. In the `FROM node:24` build stage, this means postinstall hooks in any npm dependency run as the container build user (typically root inside the Docker build context). A malicious dependency (whether injected via the disabled lockfile in `.npmrc`, version squatting, or a compromised upstream package) can: write files anywhere in the `/juice-shop` build context, overwrite the application entrypoint at `build/app.js`, or read environment variables injected as Docker `ARG`/`ENV` — including `CYCLONEDX_NPM_VERSION`. The modified build context is then copied into the final distroless stage via `COPY --from=installer --chown=65532:0 /juice-shop .` at `Dockerfile:39`, propagating any injected code into the published `bkimminich/juice-shop:latest` image.
threats.evidence_summary: `Dockerfile:5` uses `--unsafe-perm` with `npm install`, allowing lifecycle hooks to run as root and modify any file in the build context before it is copied to the production image.
threats.impact_description: A compromised npm dependency can inject code into the published Docker image shipped to all Juice Shop users and deployments.
threats.controls_in_place: The final image uses `gcr.io/distroless/nodejs24-debian13` and runs as UID 65532, limiting runtime privilege. However, the build stage runs as root with `--unsafe-perm`.
threats.mitigation_title: Remove --unsafe-perm from Dockerfile npm install and enforce lockfile in Docker build

steps: Remove `--unsafe-perm` from the `npm install` command at `Dockerfile:5`. Modern npm (v7+) does not require this flag for production installs; it was historically needed for older npm lifecycle script behaviour.

After committing `package-lock.json` (see `ci-cd-pipeline-002`), change `Dockerfile:5` to `npm ci --omit=dev` so the Docker build uses the pinned lockfile and fails on any lockfile mismatch, preventing silent dependency substitution in the build stage.
remediation.code_example:
# Dockerfile — installer stage
FROM node:24 AS installer
COPY . /juice-shop
WORKDIR /juice-shop
RUN npm i -g typescript ts-node
RUN npm ci --omit=dev    # was: npm install --omit=dev --unsafe-perm

threats.title: Hardcoded Deployment Email in Public Workflow (ci.yml)
threats.scenario: `ci.yml:342` hardcodes `heroku_email: bjoern.kimminich@owasp.org` in the Heroku deploy step. This personal email address is committed in plaintext to the public repository. While the email itself is not a credential, it is the Heroku account identity used for deployment authentication. An attacker can correlate this email with the `HEROKU_API_KEY` secret (which keys Heroku API calls to this account) to identify the blast radius of any API key compromise, attempt social engineering against Heroku support to reset credentials, or try the email in credential-stuffing attacks against other services. Additionally, the `SOLUTIONS_WEBHOOK` secret is passed as a plain environment variable to the Cypress e2e job (`ci.yml:223`); if any test's stdout or exception output contains the webhook URL, GitHub's log masking (which is exact-string-match) may not cover URL-encoded or partially printed forms.
threats.evidence_summary: `ci.yml:342` contains a hardcoded personal email address as the Heroku deployment identity, permanently visible in the public repository history.
threats.impact_description: Exposes the Heroku account identity for production/staging deployments, facilitating targeted account-takeover attempts and reducing effective secrecy of the `HEROKU_API_KEY`.
threats.controls_in_place: `HEROKU_API_KEY`, `DOCKERHUB_TOKEN`, and other secrets are stored in GitHub Secrets and not hardcoded. Only the email address itself is plaintext in the workflow.
threats.mitigation_title: Move Heroku deployment email to a GitHub Secret and audit CI log output for secret leakage

steps: Create a new GitHub repository secret `HEROKU_EMAIL` containing `bjoern.kimminich@owasp.org` and replace the hardcoded value in `ci.yml:342` with `${{ secrets.HEROKU_EMAIL }}`. This prevents future git history additions from exposing the identity and establishes a consistent pattern where all deployment identities are secret-managed.

Review GitHub Actions run logs for the e2e job to confirm that `SOLUTIONS_WEBHOOK` URL does not appear in test output; if it does, update Cypress configuration to suppress webhook URL logging or use a masked environment variable wrapper.

threats.title: Archived Action Dependency in Release Pipeline (release.yml)
threats.scenario: `release.yml:66` uses `dawidd6/action-get-tag@727a6f0a561be04e09013531e73a3983a65e3479` with a comment `# v1.1.0 TODO Action is archived and should be replaced eventually`. The action repository is archived, meaning it receives no security updates, bug fixes, or maintenance. While the SHA pin prevents tag-pointer attacks, the pinned code itself may contain vulnerabilities that will never be patched. This action runs in the release pipeline — the most privileged context in the repo (triggered by `push` to `v*` tags, producing artifacts uploaded to GitHub Releases and Docker images tagged with the version string). If the archived action's code has a vulnerability (e.g. insecure handling of the tag output, log injection via specially crafted tag names), it can be exploited to corrupt the release artifact naming or inject malicious content into `GITHUB_ENV`. The `TODO` comment acknowledges the risk without a remediation timeline.
threats.evidence_summary: `release.yml:66` declares a known-archived action with a `TODO` comment acknowledging it needs replacement, used in the highest-privilege release pipeline job.
threats.impact_description: Vulnerabilities in the unmaintained `dawidd6/action-get-tag` action in the release pipeline can corrupt artifact naming or inject values into `GITHUB_ENV` used by subsequent Docker push steps.
threats.controls_in_place: The action is SHA-pinned, preventing tag-pointer hijacking. The `TODO` comment acknowledges the maintenance gap.
threats.mitigation_title: Replace archived dawidd6/action-get-tag with a maintained alternative in release.yml

steps: Replace `dawidd6/action-get-tag` with the built-in `github.ref_name` context variable, which is available in GitHub Actions when the workflow is triggered by a tag push. This eliminates the external action dependency entirely: `echo "TAG_NAME=${{ github.ref_name }}" >> $GITHUB_ENV`.
Remove the `id: tag` step and update the subsequent `tags:` reference from `${{ steps.tag.outputs.tag }}` to `${{ github.ref_name }}` in the Docker build-push step.
remediation.code_example:
# release.yml — docker job, replace archived action with context variable
- name: "Get tag name"
  run: echo "TAG_NAME=${{ github.ref_name }}" >> $GITHUB_ENV
# Then in build-push-action:
# tags: bkimminich/juice-shop:${{ env.TAG_NAME }}
